items.html:
More than accidents

An accident such as a fall or a scald is only one kind of incident you might have to report. In adult social care, an incident is any unplanned event that did, or could have, caused harm to a person you support, a colleague, a visitor or yourself. Some are obvious; many are not. Recognising the full range matters, because an event you do not treat as an incident is one you will not record, and one that nobody can learn from. If you are ever in doubt, report it and let a senior decide.

Common types in care

  • Medication errors: a missed dose, wrong dose, wrong person or wrong time.
  • Safeguarding incidents: suspected abuse, neglect or a disclosure of harm.
  • Missed or late visits: a call not made can leave someone without food, medication or help.
  • Going missing or absconding: a person leaving unnoticed and at risk.
  • Behaviour that challenges: aggression or distress that risks harm to someone.
  • Data and IT incidents: a lost work phone, a care plan left on a bus, or notes emailed to the wrong address.

Pressure ulcers, unexplained injuries and equipment failures count too. Several of these may be events your manager has to report onward, so none should be brushed aside as ‘just one of those things’.

items.question: A carer arrives in the morning to find yesterday evening’s medication still sitting untaken in the blister pack. Is this an incident?

answers.text: Yes — a missed dose is a medication error, an incident in its own right, and must be recorded and reported even though no harm is yet obvious.
answers.feedback: Correct. A missed dose is a recognised medication error and needs recording and reporting so the person can be checked and the cause looked into.

answers.text: No, because the person seems well this morning, so there is nothing to record.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Harm from a missed dose may appear later, and a near miss still has to be recorded so it can be reviewed.

answers.text: No, medication mistakes are only incidents if the person is taken to hospital.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A medication error is an incident regardless of the outcome; the outcome affects what happens next, not whether it is reported.

answers.text: Only the next carer needs to know; it does not need writing down.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A quiet word is not enough; a medication error must be formally recorded and escalated.

items.html:
People come first

When something goes wrong, your first duty is to the people involved, not to the paperwork. Stay calm, make the immediate area safe, and check whether anyone is hurt. Give first aid within the limits of your training, and if someone is seriously injured, unresponsive, or you are simply unsure, call 999 without delay. Do not move a person who may have a spinal or hip injury unless they are in immediate danger. Reassure the person and anyone who witnessed the event, and stay with them until help arrives.

Preserve the scene and raise the alarm

Once people are safe, protect any evidence about what happened. If a piece of equipment failed or a substance was involved, leave things as they are where you safely can, and do not clean up or repair anything until a senior has seen it — this matters especially where the event may need reporting to the regulator or the police. Then tell the right people, in the right order:

  • Emergency services: for anything life-threatening.
  • Your manager or on-call senior: as soon as the person is safe.
  • Family or next of kin: as your manager directs.

Record what you saw and did as soon as you can, while it is fresh in your mind.

items.question: After a fall involving a hoist sling that tore, you should tidy the area and put the damaged sling back in the store cupboard before your manager arrives, to keep things neat.
items.correctMsg: Correct — this is false. Faulty equipment involved in an incident should be left as it is, taken out of use and kept for inspection, because it may be evidence for an investigation or a report to the regulator.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite — this is false. You should never tidy away or reuse equipment involved in an incident; leave it as it is and take it out of use so it can be inspected.

items.html:
Stick to the facts

A good incident report records what actually happened, in plain, objective language. Write down what you saw, heard and did, in the order it happened, using the person’s own words in quotation marks where you can. Keep opinion and guesswork out of it. ‘I found Mr Ali sitting on the floor beside his bed’ is a fact; ‘Mr Ali fell because he was being careless’ is an assumption you did not witness. Avoid labels and avoid blaming anyone. If you genuinely must record an opinion, make it clear that is what it is.

Timely, complete and signed

Write your report as soon as possible while your memory is accurate, ideally before the end of your shift. Include the date, the exact time, the location, who was present, and what injuries or effects you noticed. A body map — an outline of the body on which you mark the site, size and appearance of any injury — helps you describe bruises or wounds precisely without handling the person unnecessarily. Do not leave gaps or use correction fluid; if you make a mistake, cross it out with a single line, initial it, and write the correction alongside. Sign and date the report, because it is a legal record that may later be read by managers, the CQC, families or a court.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete this guidance on writing an incident report.
items.textField: A report should record what you saw and did as *facts*, not opinions, and be written while your memory is still *accurate*. Use a *body* map to show exactly where an injury is, and never use correction *fluid* to hide a mistake. Finally, *sign* the report, because it is a legal record.

items.html:
Some events must reach the CQC

Registered providers in England have a legal duty to notify the Care Quality Commission about certain serious events, so that the regulator can see what is happening and act if it needs to. Your manager or the registered manager makes these notifications, not you as an individual worker — but you play a vital part by reporting promptly and recording clearly, because they can only notify what they know about. These notifications sit alongside, and are separate from, any RIDDOR report to the HSE and any safeguarding referral to the local authority.

Notifiable events

Among the events a provider must tell the CQC about are:

  • the death of a person using the service;
  • a serious injury to a person using the service;
  • abuse, or an allegation of abuse, concerning a person using the service;
  • an application or authorisation to deprive a person of their liberty;
  • any event that stops, or may stop, the service running safely.

Where a death is unexpected, unexplained or may be linked to the care given, it can also have to be reported to the coroner, who may investigate and hold an inquest. You do not decide these things on your own; your job is to escalate quickly so that those responsible can meet the timescales.

items.question: A person you support dies suddenly and unexpectedly during your shift. Beyond giving emergency care, what is the right understanding of reporting?

answers.text: You escalate to your manager immediately and record what happened; the registered manager notifies the CQC, and an unexpected death may also be reported to the coroner.
answers.feedback: Correct. Your part is to act, escalate and record accurately; the registered manager makes the CQC notification, and an unexpected death may involve the coroner.

answers.text: You must ring the CQC yourself straight away, or you will be personally fined.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The notification is the registered provider’s duty, not the individual worker’s; your duty is to escalate and record promptly.

answers.text: Nothing needs reporting to any regulator, because death is a natural part of care work.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The death of a person using the service is a notifiable event, and an unexpected death may also concern the coroner.

answers.text: You should wait until the family has been told before writing anything down.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Record the facts as soon as you can while they are fresh; informing the family is important but does not delay accurate recording.

items.html:
Look for causes, not culprits

Reporting an incident is only worthwhile if something is learned from it. After a serious or recurring incident, your service will investigate to understand what happened and why. A good investigation looks for the root cause — the underlying reason — rather than for someone to blame. Most incidents come from a chain of factors such as staffing, training, equipment, procedures or communication, not simply one careless person. A no-blame, open culture encourages people to report honestly, including their own mistakes, because they know the aim is to prevent harm rather than to punish.

Being open and changing practice

Openness is also a legal duty. Under the duty of candour (Regulation 20), providers must be honest with people and their families when something goes wrong and causes certain harm — telling them what happened, apologising, and explaining what will be done. Learning must then change practice: updating a care plan, repairing or replacing equipment, revising a procedure or arranging training. Consider Mrs Okafor, who was given another resident’s tablets. The investigation found the two had similar names and the medication trolley was crowded and rushed at breakfast. The service added photographs to the medication records and calmed the morning routine — a change that protects everyone, prompted by one honest report.

items.question: Your service uses a ‘no-blame’ approach to incident reporting. What does this mean in practice?

answers.text: Staff are encouraged to report incidents and their own mistakes honestly, so the real causes can be found and put right, rather than facing automatic punishment.
answers.feedback: Correct. A no-blame culture is about honest reporting and finding root causes; it makes services safer by helping mistakes come to light.

answers.text: Nobody is ever accountable, and even serious misconduct is simply ignored.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. No-blame does not remove accountability for genuine misconduct; it removes the fear that stops honest reporting of ordinary mistakes.

answers.text: Incidents are recorded but never investigated, so that staff are not upset.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Incidents are still investigated; the point is to learn from them and change practice, not to avoid looking at them.

answers.text: Only managers may report incidents, so that frontline staff are shielded from blame.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Everyone is encouraged to report; limiting reporting to managers would hide exactly the events the service needs to learn from.
